title: What grit for cedar before staining?
problem: Soft cedar can show sanding scratches or absorb stain unevenly when the surface is over-sanded or left rough.
likely_cause: Cedar is soft, so coarse scratches show easily and very fine sanding can reduce stain absorption.
recommended_grit: Use 150 then 180 for most cedar stain prep. Use 220 only for very smooth finish prep.
steps: Sand with the grain using 150 grit.
Move to 180 grit for final stain prep.
Clean dust from the pores and surface.
Test stain on a hidden area or scrap piece.
avoid: Do not use heavy pressure on soft cedar.
success_check: The cedar feels smooth, has no deep scratch lines, and still accepts stain evenly.
quick_answer: For cedar before staining, use 150 then 180. Use 220 only when the surface needs a smoother final prep.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not start with 80 grit unless removing heavy damage.
Do not polish cedar with ultra-fine grits before stain.
search_phrases: what grit for cedar before staining

title: What grit for teak before oil?
problem: Teak can look patchy or dull if the surface is not prepared evenly before oil.
likely_cause: Teak is dense and oily, so sanding dust and uneven scratch patterns can affect the final look.
recommended_grit: Use 180 then 220 before oil. For refinishing weathered teak, start with 120 only where needed.
steps: Use 120 only on weathered or rough areas.
Remove dust thoroughly before applying oil.
avoid: Do not oil over sanding dust or grey oxidized wood.
success_check: The teak has an even color and smooth feel before oiling.
quick_answer: For teak before oil, use 180 then 220. Start with 120 only on weathered or rough areas.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not over-sand one area and create color patches.
Do not apply oil before cleaning dust.
search_phrases: what grit for teak before oil

title: What grit for maple before staining?
problem: Maple can stain unevenly and show sanding marks when prep is not controlled.
likely_cause: Maple is dense and can absorb stain unevenly, especially when sanding is inconsistent.
recommended_grit: Use 180 then 220. Keep the sanding pattern uniform before applying stain or conditioner.
steps: Test stain on scrap or a hidden area first.
avoid: Do not leave uneven grit patterns on maple before staining.
success_check: The maple surface is smooth and evenly sanded with no shiny or rough patches.
quick_answer: For maple before staining, use 180 then 220 and keep the sanding pattern uniform.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not over-sand small spots.
Do not stain before removing dust.
search_phrases: what grit for maple before staining

title: What grit for live edge wood?
problem: Live edge slabs can have rough bark lines, saw marks, and uneven areas that need different sanding pressure.
likely_cause: Flat areas and natural edges need different handling so the edge is not destroyed while the surface is leveled.
recommended_grit: Use 80 or 120 for rough saw marks, then 150, 180, and 220 for finish prep.
steps: Start flat areas with 80 or 120 only where rough.
Hand sand the natural edge lightly.
avoid: Do not flatten or erase the natural edge with heavy sanding.
success_check: The slab top is smooth while the natural edge still keeps its shape and texture.
quick_answer: For live edge wood, level rough areas with 80 or 120, then finish with 150, 180, and 220.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not use the same pressure on the natural edge as the flat face.
Do not skip intermediate grits on visible slab tops.
search_phrases: what grit for live edge wood

title: How to avoid sanding through veneer
problem: Thin veneer can sand through quickly and expose the layer underneath.
likely_cause: Veneer has a thin surface layer, so coarse grit and heavy pressure remove material too fast.
recommended_grit: Use 220 for light prep. Use 320 for gentle scuff sanding on already smooth veneer.
steps: Stop as soon as the surface is dull and smooth.
avoid: Do not use 80 or 120 grit on thin veneer faces.
success_check: The veneer is lightly scuffed with no exposed substrate or pale sand-through spots.
quick_answer: To avoid sanding through veneer, use 220 lightly. Use 320 for gentle scuffing on smooth veneer.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not use coarse grit on thin veneer.
Do not sand one spot longer than the surrounding area.
search_phrases: how to avoid sanding through veneer

title: What grit for melamine before painting?
problem: Paint can peel from melamine if the slick surface is not scuffed correctly.
likely_cause: Melamine has a hard, smooth surface that needs cleaning and light abrasion before primer.
recommended_grit: Use 220 or 320 to scuff the surface before bonding primer.
steps: Apply a primer made for slick surfaces.
avoid: Do not paint glossy melamine without cleaning and scuff sanding.
success_check: The melamine is clean, dull, and evenly scuffed before primer.
quick_answer: For melamine before painting, clean first, then scuff with 220 or 320 before bonding primer.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not use coarse grit that gouges the surface.
Do not skip primer on slick melamine.
search_phrases: what grit for melamine before painting

title: What grit between chalk paint coats?
problem: Chalk paint can feel gritty or show brush marks between coats.
likely_cause: Light sanding smooths dust nibs and brush texture without removing the previous coat.
recommended_grit: Use 320 or 400 lightly between fully dry coats.
steps: Apply the next coat or topcoat.
avoid: Do not press hard or sand through the chalk paint layer.
success_check: The surface feels smooth and powdery dust is removed before the next coat.
quick_answer: Between chalk paint coats, sand lightly with 320 or 400 after the coat is fully dry.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not sand wet or soft chalk paint.
Do not leave dust before topcoat.
search_phrases: what grit between chalk paint coats

title: What grit between enamel paint coats?
problem: Enamel paint can show dust nibs or poor adhesion between coats if the surface is not lightly scuffed.
likely_cause: Fully cured enamel is smoother and harder, so the next coat may need light sanding for tooth.
recommended_grit: Use 400 or 600 lightly between cured enamel coats.
wet_or_dry: Dry for normal scuff sanding; wet only when the paint instructions allow it.
steps: Let the enamel cure as directed.
Apply the next coat over a clean dull surface.
avoid: Do not sand enamel while it is still soft or tacky.
success_check: The surface is smooth, clean, and lightly dulled before recoating.
quick_answer: Between enamel paint coats, use 400 or 600 lightly after the previous coat has cured.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not use coarse grit between finish coats.
Do not recoat over sanding dust.
search_phrases: what grit between enamel paint coats
smooth enamel paint before next coat

title: What grit to sand oil-based primer?
problem: Oil-based primer can show brush marks, dust nibs, or roughness before paint.
likely_cause: Primer needs to cure before sanding; sanding too early can clog paper and smear the surface.
recommended_grit: Use 320 or 400 after the primer is fully cured. Use 600 for finer topcoat prep.
steps: Let the primer cure as directed.
Sand with 320 or 400 using light pressure.
avoid: Do not sand oil-based primer while it is soft.
success_check: The primer powders cleanly and feels smooth before painting.
quick_answer: For oil-based primer, use 320 or 400 after it cures. Use 600 for finer topcoat prep.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not force sanding if primer gums up.
Do not paint over primer dust.
search_phrases: what grit to sand oil based primer
sand oil based primer before paint

title: What grit between latex paint coats?
problem: Latex paint can feel rough or show dust between coats.
likely_cause: Light sanding removes nibs after the coat dries, but sanding too early can peel or gum the paint.
recommended_grit: Use 320 or 400 lightly after latex paint dries fully.
steps: Let the latex paint dry fully.
Apply the next coat over the clean surface.
avoid: Do not sand latex paint while it is rubbery or soft.
success_check: The coat feels smooth and dust-free before the next coat.
quick_answer: Between latex paint coats, use 320 or 400 lightly after the paint is fully dry.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not use heavy pressure on latex paint.
Do not leave sanding dust before recoating.
search_phrases: what grit between latex paint coats
smooth latex paint before next coat

title: What grit to level high-build primer?
problem: High-build primer can have texture, waves, or guide-coat marks that need leveling.
likely_cause: High-build primer is designed to be sanded, but it must be leveled with a grit that cuts without leaving deep final scratches.
recommended_grit: Use 320 for leveling, then 400 or 600 before paint.
wet_or_dry: Dry for leveling; wet at finer stages only when the coating system allows it.
steps: Block sand with 320 to level texture.
avoid: Do not paint over unlevel primer texture.
success_check: The primer is level, smooth, and has a uniform scratch pattern before paint.
quick_answer: For high-build primer, level with 320, then refine with 400 or 600 before paint.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not use fingertips to level flat primer panels.

title: What grit to sand paint drips on a wall?
problem: Paint drips on a wall can leave raised ridges after drying.
likely_cause: A drip must be leveled before repainting, then refined so the repair does not show through the next coat.
recommended_grit: Use 220 to level dried drips, then 320 for smoother repaint prep.
avoid: Do not sand wet or soft paint drips.
success_check: The drip ridge is flat and the repaired area blends with the surrounding wall.
quick_answer: For dried wall paint drips, level with 220, then refine with 320 before repainting.

title: What grit fixes a paint tape edge?
problem: A raised paint edge from masking tape can remain visible after the tape is removed.
likely_cause: The edge needs to be feathered gently so the next coat blends instead of showing a ridge.
recommended_grit: Use 320 to feather the edge, then 400 or 600 for finer repaint prep.
avoid: Do not dig a groove along the tape line.
success_check: The paint edge feels smooth and blends into the surrounding surface.
quick_answer: To fix a paint tape edge, feather with 320, then refine with 400 or 600 before repainting.

title: What grit removes primer overspray?
problem: Primer overspray can leave a rough dusty texture around the repair area.
likely_cause: Dry primer dust can sit on the surface and needs light sanding without cutting into the surrounding finish.
recommended_grit: Use 600 for light primer overspray on prepared surfaces. Use 400 first only for heavier texture before repainting.

avoid: Do not sand through surrounding finish while removing overspray.

quick_answer: For primer overspray, use 600 lightly. Use 400 first only on heavier texture before repainting.

title: What grit removes clear coat overspray?
problem: Clear coat overspray can leave a rough texture on the surface.
likely_cause: Overspray sits on top of the finish and should be refined carefully to avoid cutting through the clear coat.
recommended_grit: Use 1500 or 2000 wet sanding for light overspray, then 3000 before polishing.
avoid: Do not start with coarse grit on clear coat overspray.
success_check: The rough overspray is gone and the surface has an even fine haze before polishing.

title: What grit for galvanized metal before painting?
problem: Paint may not stick well to galvanized metal if the surface stays slick or contaminated.

avoid: Do not grind through the galvanized coating with coarse grit.

title: What grit to smooth welds?
problem: Welds can leave high spots, sharp edges, and coarse grinding marks.

recommended_grit: Use 80 or 120 for shaping, then 180, 220, and 320 for blending before paint.

steps: Finish with 320 before primer or paint.
avoid: Do not jump from rough weld cleanup directly to paint.
success_check: The weld area is smooth, blended, and has an even scratch pattern.
quick_answer: For smoothing welds, shape with 80 or 120, then blend with 180, 220, and 320.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not remove too much surrounding metal.

title: What grit for pitted rust?
problem: Pitted rust leaves dark low spots even after the surface is sanded.
likely_cause: Pitting means corrosion has eaten into the metal, so sanding can remove loose rust but may not erase deep pits without removing metal.
recommended_grit: Use 80 or 120 to remove loose rust, then 180, 220, and 320 before primer.
steps: Sand with 80 or 120 to cut corrosion.
Clean and prime the metal promptly.
avoid: Do not assume fine grit will remove deep pits.
success_check: The loose rust is gone and the remaining surface is clean enough for primer.
quick_answer: For pitted rust, use 80 or 120 to remove loose rust, then refine with 180, 220, and 320 before primer.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not leave rust dust before primer.

title: What grit for motorcycle tank paint prep?

quick_answer: For motorcycle tank paint prep, use 320 or 400. Use 600 for finer topcoat prep.

Do not paint over fuel residue, wax, or sanding dust.

avoid: Do not leave glossy spots before painting.

title: What grit for ABS plastic parts?

title: Can I sand vinyl plastic?
problem: Vinyl plastic can become scratched, fuzzy, or damaged when sanded aggressively.
likely_cause: Flexible vinyl often does not sand like hard plastic; it can smear or texture instead of cutting cleanly.
recommended_grit: Use 600 or finer only for very light scuffing on hard vinyl surfaces. Avoid sanding soft flexible vinyl.
wet_or_dry: Dry for light scuffing; wet only on hard surfaces where residue can be controlled.
steps: Identify whether the vinyl is hard or flexible.
Use 600 or finer only for light scuffing on hard vinyl.
Stop if the surface smears or gets fuzzy.
avoid: Do not use coarse sandpaper on soft flexible vinyl.

title: What grit for polycarbonate?

For visible scratch repair, avoid sanding the finished laminate face.

Clean thoroughly before primer or coating.
avoid: Do not use coarse grit on finished laminate countertops.

problem: Porcelain tub surfaces can be permanently scratched by regular sandpaper.

recommended_grit: Do not use standard sandpaper for normal porcelain tub cleaning or shine restoration.
